turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
give in-depth references for the following text using harvard referencing style in a list –   Implementation of a lifetime cap on non-concessional contributions: 
The government might contemplate instituting a lifetime cap on non-concessional contributions, thereby circumscribing the sum individuals can contribute to their superannuation after-tax throughout their lives. This potential alteration could affect Kate and Emily, as it would curtail opportunities for substantial after-tax contributions to their superannuation, primarily during instances of windfalls, asset sales, or inheritance. Contingent on the cap amount and their financial tactics, this adjustment could either restrict their capacity to augment their superannuation balances prior to retirement or prompt them to concentrate on saving and investing within other tax-efficient structures beyond superannuation to achieve their retirement aspirations.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
1. Australian Government (2016) Budget 2016-17: Introducing a transfer balance cap of $1.6 million for account-based pensions. [online] Available at: http://www.budget.gov.au/2016-17/content/glossies/tax_super/html/tax_super-07.htm [Accessed 15 Sep. 2021].

2. Australian Government (2016) The Treasury: Objective of superannuation. [online] Available at: https://treasury.gov.au/sites/default/files/2019-03/C2016-044_Rice-Warner-FSI-Review-response-A.pdf [Accessed 15 Sep. 2021].

3. Evans, C., Sorensen, K., and Brimble, M. (2018) The Concessional and Non-Concessional Contribution Caps for Retirement Savings: Are They Inefficient and Inequitable? Australian Tax Forum, 33(3), pp. 491-536.

4. Kingston, G., and Piggott, J. (2017) Superannuation policy for post-retirement: Lifetime annuities and lifetime non-concessional contribution caps. The Economics Record, 94(304), pp. 1-18. 

5. O'Connell, A. (2017) Consequences of a lifetime non-concessional cap on superannuation contributions. Taxation Institute of Australia - The Tax Adviser, November, pp. 10-14.

6. Warren, D. (2020) Superannuation and wealth accumulation over the life cycle: An empirical evaluation of proposed policy reforms. Australian Economic Review, 53(1), pp. 52-72.

7. Yates, J. (2015) Risk, saving, and financial (in)security in late working life: Empirical evidence for the impact of the financial crisis on financial well-being. Australian Journal of Social Issues, 50(4), pp. 369-389.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
give in-depth references for the following text using harvard referencing style in a list –   
This amendment fortifies sanctions for corporate and financial sector misbehaviour by modifying the Corporations Act 2001, National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009, and Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act 2001. Its primary objective is to deter wrongdoings and enhance public confidence in the financial system. It responds to the Enforcement Review Taskforce’s recommendations by amplifying criminal and civil penalties for violations, streamlining criminal and civil penalties, and ensuring regulators possess the requisite tools to combat misconduct efficaciously.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:

2. Australian Government (2019) Corporations Amendment (Strengthening Protections for Employee Entitlements) Bill 2018. [online] Available at: https://www.aph.gov.au/Parliamentary_Business/Bills_Legislation/Bills_Search_Results/Result?bId=r6158 [Accessed 15 Sep. 2021].

4. Comino, V., and Fenwick, H. (2019) Strengthening corporate and financial sector penalties: The reforms introduced by the Treasury Laws Amendment (strengthening corporate and financial sector penalties) Act 2018. Company and Securities Law Journal, 37(1), pp. 4-24.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: give in-depth references for the following text using harvard referencing style in a list –   This amendment establishes a framework for design and distribution responsibilities concerning financial products and augments ASIC’s intervention capabilities. It revises the Corporations Act 2001, National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009, and the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act 2001. Its purpose is to address consumer detriment by ensuring that financial products are directed and sold to suitable consumers, with financial product providers and distributors instituting proper consumer protection measures.

turns.speaker: assistant
